---
title: "Optimization: finding the transform"
---

A registration is a minimisation: pick the transform parameters $\theta$ that make the warped moving
image look most like the fixed image. This chapter owns the machinery that *finds* $\theta$ for the
*linear* transforms (rigid, affine) and the coarse-to-fine / convergence scaffolding every recipe
shares. The one idea it adds is that the structure of the objective dictates the solver. A
sum-of-squares cost exposes a residual *vector*, which earns a cheap second-order step (Gauss–Newton /
Levenberg–Marquardt, matrix-free on the GPU); a scalar similarity (LNCC / MI / correlation-ratio) has no
residual, so the solver falls back to first-order BFGS. Either is wrapped in a Gaussian-pyramid
continuation (coarse, smooth levels for capture range; fine levels to sharpen), each finer level
warm-started from the coarser solution, and - when the template is fixed - the template is linearised
*once* and that linearisation reused every iteration (the inverse-compositional fast path, about 4–7×
faster).

A second thread runs underneath: the optimiser does not move on the curved transform group, it moves on
the flat *Lie algebra* parameters $\theta$ that the [transforms chapter](transforms.qmd#sec-lie-exp)
charted by $\exp$. Every step is a vector in that tangent space, the warp Jacobian differentiates the
$\exp$ chart, and the inverse-compositional update composes the increment back onto the group. The
unit's group-theoretic framing is what makes the search unconstrained and well-conditioned.

Everything in the unit so far has set up the *ingredients* of a registration: a parametrised family of
transforms (a [chart on the transform manifold](transforms.qmd#sec-transform-manifold)), a way to
[apply one](resampling.qmd#sec-pullback-sampling) (resampling the moving image at the mapped grid), and
a [similarity score](similarity.qmd#sec-ssd) for the result. This chapter is where they meet the
optimiser. The governing idea of the unit - *a registration is a map between two geometries, found by
minimising a mismatch energy over a parametrised family of transforms* - becomes, here, a concrete
numerical loop.

**A motivating example.** You call `rigid_register(moving, fixed)` to realign one fMRI volume to a
reference, or to align a subject's T1 to an atlas. Six numbers come back (three rotations, three
translations) and an aligned image. How? The obvious recipe - write down the cost as a function of those
six numbers and run gradient descent - works, eventually, but it is slow and fragile: the cost surface
is full of local minima far from the answer, and even near the answer the rotation and translation
directions have wildly different curvature, so a single step size either crawls or diverges. This
chapter builds the real solver one fix at a time, motivating each rung.

The code lives in `register/_core.py` (the driver), `register/_objective.py` (the objective protocol),
`register/_inverse_compositional.py` (the fast path), `register/_converge.py` (the iteration
scaffolding), and `linalg/optimize.py` (the generic least-squares solvers).

## The objective: $\theta \mapsto \text{cost}$ {#sec-objective}

The first thing to nail down is *what* we minimise. The optimiser does not know or care that $\theta$ is
a transform - it sees a function from a parameter vector to a number. So we wrap the registration in
exactly that interface. In `register/_objective.py` it is the `Objective` protocol (a
`runtime_checkable` `Protocol`):

- `cost(params) -> scalar` - the dissimilarity at these parameters, lower is better;
- `residual(params) -> vector` - the per-voxel residual $r(\theta)$ whose $\tfrac12\lVert r\rVert^2$ *is*
  the cost, when one exists;
- `is_least_squares` (a property) - whether `residual` is available.

That last flag is the hinge of the whole chapter. *The structure of the objective routes the solver.* If
the cost is a sum of squares, `residual` returns the vector being squared, and we can take a
second-order-ish step almost for free (@sec-gauss-newton). If it is a scalar with no natural residual
vector (LNCC / MI / correlation-ratio), we fall back to first-order BFGS (@sec-bfgs). The optimiser
dispatch is written *once* against `Objective`, so a brand-new objective is a single record, not a new
branch in the driver.

The image-pair workhorse is `MetricObjective` (a `@dataclass(frozen=True)`), which closes over four
things:

```text
MetricObjective(metric, warp, fixed, mask)
    cost(θ)     = metric.cost(warp(θ), fixed, mask=mask)
    residual(θ) = metric.residual(warp(θ), fixed, mask=mask)
    is_least_squares = metric.is_least_squares
```

`warp` is the level's closure `params -> warped` (build the sampling grid from $\theta$ through the
`model.exp` chart, resample `moving`); `fixed` is the reference at this resolution; `mask` (optional) is
a per-voxel weight on the fixed grid. The metric's own `is_least_squares` flag (`True` for
[`SSD`](similarity.qmd#sec-ssd), `False` for [`LNCC`/`MI`/`CR`](similarity.qmd#sec-lncc)) is what
`MetricObjective` re-exposes - so SSD routes to Gauss–Newton and the rest to BFGS, automatically. The
objective abstraction earns its keep elsewhere too: the boundary-based registration cost (BBR;
`_bbr.BoundaryObjective`) is a *sibling* `Objective` with *no fixed image* - it scores $\theta$ directly
over surface-point samples - and reuses the very same optimiser dispatch.

### The driver and its configuration

The objective is built and minimised per pyramid level by `multi_resolution_register`
(`register/_core.py`), the coarse-to-fine driver shared by `rigid_register` / `affine_register`. Its
static configuration is the `RegistrationSpec` (a frozen, hashable dataclass, so it rides `jit` static
arguments - the [`Interpolator`](resampling.qmd#sec-kernels) precedent). The fields you will set:

| field | what it controls |
|---|---|
| `levels` | number of Gaussian-pyramid resolutions (default 3) |
| `iterations` | optimiser steps per level: an `int` (same at every level) or a length-`levels` **coarse-to-fine** tuple |
| `metric` | the similarity record (`SSD()`, `LNCC(radius=…)`, `MI(bins=…)`, `CorrelationRatio(bins=…)`) — carries its own hyper-parameters |
| `optimizer` | `'auto'` (least-squares → `'lm'`, else → `'bfgs'`), or force `'lm'` / `'gn'` / `'bfgs'` |
| `interpolation` | the warp's sampling kernel (an `Interpolator`) |
| `boundary_mode`, `cval` | out-of-bounds handling (default zero-fill) |
| `pyramid_factor`, `pyramid_sigma` | downsample factor and anti-alias $\sigma$ |
| `cg_tol` | inner-CG tolerance for the GN/LM normal-equation solve |
| `mode` | iteration strategy — `'fixed'` (the `lax.scan`) or `'early_exit'` (the `lax.while_loop`); see @sec-convergence |
| `convergence` | the `Convergence(threshold, window)` parameterising `mode='early_exit'` (inert under `'fixed'`) |
| `ic_line_search` | the opt-in cost-decrease guard on the inverse-compositional step (@sec-inverse-comp) |

The result is a `RegistrationResult` `NamedTuple` with four fields. `matrix` is the estimated homogeneous
transform, *self-contained* - the centre the warp applies it about is baked in, so
`apply_affine(coords, matrix)` reproduces the warp and the matrix composes with another result. (In
`IndexSpace` it is the full-resolution fixed-voxel → moving-voxel index map; in `WorldSpace` the
fixed-world → moving-world transform in mm.) `params` holds the raw *about-origin* Lie parameters at full
resolution; `warped` is `moving` resampled onto the fixed grid; `cost_history` is the concatenated
per-level optimiser cost traces.

One small but load-bearing helper is `resolve_iterations(iterations, levels)` (**HOST-CTOR** - it runs
on the host, returning a Python list). It expands the schedule to one count per level, *finest first*: an
`int` becomes `[n] * levels`; a length-`levels` sequence is taken in the natural *coarse-to-fine* order
the user writes it and *reversed*, because the driver loops the pyramid finest-index-0. This lets you
front-load the cheap coarse levels and starve the expensive finest one - a second-order solver converges
in a handful of finest-level steps, so a schedule like `iterations=(40, 20, 5)` (coarse → fine) is both
faster and just as accurate as a flat `30`.

## Coarse-to-fine: the pyramid as a continuation method {#sec-coarse-fine}

This is where naive gradient descent first breaks. Run the optimiser directly on the full-resolution
cost and it will, more often than not, settle into a *local* minimum: a texture ridge in the fixed image
lines up with a different texture ridge in the moving image, the cost is locally minimal, and the iterate
is stuck far from the true alignment. The cost surface over $\theta$ is bumpy, and the bumps are
precisely the fine image detail.

The cure is a *continuation method*: instead of optimising the hard problem directly, solve a sequence
of progressively harder problems, each warm-started from the last. We get the sequence for free from the
[Gaussian pyramid](resampling.qmd#sec-pyramid). At a coarse level the image is heavily blurred and
downsampled, which *erases the fine detail* - and with it the local minima. The coarse cost surface is
smooth and has one wide basin around the true answer (the *capture range* is large), so even a poor
starting guess slides into it. We optimise there, then move to the next finer level, *warm-starting it
from the coarse solution*. The finer level adds detail back - the basin narrows and grows local wiggles -
but we now start near the bottom of the right basin, so the optimiser only has to sharpen, not search.

@fig-optimization-basins makes this concrete. The same SSD-versus-translation cost is plotted at three
pyramid levels. The coarse level (blue) is smooth: its flanks are wiggle-free, so an optimiser anywhere
on them rolls cleanly toward the true displacement. The finer levels keep the high-frequency texture,
which shows up as local bumps on the flanks - minima a gradient method would get trapped in *if it
started there cold*. All three share the same global minimum, which is the whole point: the coarse level
finds the basin, the fine level pinpoints the bottom.

```{python}
#| label: fig-optimization-basins
#| fig-cap: "Coarse-to-fine basin widening. The same SSD cost as a function of a trial translation, at three Gaussian-pyramid levels. The coarse (heavily blurred) level has smooth flanks — a wide, trap-free capture range — while the fine level keeps the texture that creates local minima. All share the true-displacement minimum: optimise coarse to find the basin, then fine to sharpen."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# A 1-D intensity profile (the "fixed" image): two bumps + texture, so the
# SSD-vs-translation cost has a wide global basin AND fine local wiggles.
N = 256
x = np.arange(N)
def bump(c, w):
    return np.exp(-0.5 * ((x - c) / w) ** 2)
fixed = 1.0 * bump(90, 14) + 0.8 * bump(165, 10)
fixed += 0.10 * np.sin(x / 3.0)          # high-frequency texture -> local minima
fixed += 0.04 * rng.standard_normal(N)   # a little noise

def gaussian_blur_1d(sig, sigma):
    if sigma <= 0:
        return sig.copy()
    r = int(np.ceil(3 * sigma))
    k = np.exp(-0.5 * (np.arange(-r, r + 1) / sigma) ** 2)
    k /= k.sum()
    return np.convolve(sig, k, mode="same")

# Coarse levels are heavily smoothed (the anti-alias blur of the pyramid).
sigmas = {"coarse (level 2)": 9.0, "medium (level 1)": 3.0, "fine (level 0)": 0.0}

true_shift = 24
shifts = np.arange(-70, 71)
colors = {"coarse (level 2)": "#3b6fb0", "medium (level 1)": "#d08a2c",
          "fine (level 0)": "#b03b4b"}

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
for name, sigma in sigmas.items():
    f = gaussian_blur_1d(fixed, sigma)
    m = gaussian_blur_1d(np.roll(fixed, true_shift), sigma)
    cost = []
    for s in shifts:
        warped = np.roll(m, -s)               # try to undo the shift
        valid = slice(80, N - 80)             # ignore wrap-around edges
        cost.append(np.mean((warped[valid] - f[valid]) ** 2))
    cost = np.asarray(cost)
    cost = cost / cost.max()                   # normalise for overlay
    ax.plot(shifts, cost, label=name, color=colors[name], lw=2.0)

ax.axvline(true_shift, color="0.4", ls="--", lw=1.2, label="true displacement")
ax.set_xlabel("trial translation (voxels)")
ax.set_ylabel("SSD cost (normalised)")
ax.set_title("The same objective at three pyramid levels")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
ax.set_ylim(0, 1.05)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The driver loops the pyramid from the coarsest level (highest index) to the finest (index 0),
rebuilding the `MetricObjective` at each resolution and warm-starting $\theta$ from the previous level's
optimum.

### Rescaling parameters across levels

Warm-starting hides a subtlety: a parameter that means "move 3 voxels" at a coarse 16-voxel grid means
"move 6 voxels" at the 32-voxel grid below it. So the warm-start must *rescale*, and *which* parameters
scale depends on the coordinate space.

This is the responsibility of `model.rescale_to_grid(params, ratio)` on the
[`TransformModel`](transforms.qmd#sec-fit) (`Rigid` / `Affine`), where `ratio` is the per-axis
$\text{finer\_shape} / \text{coarser\_shape}$. Both `Rigid` and `Affine` lay the parameter vector out as
*linear-block-first, translation-last*, so the shared rescale (`_rescale_translation_last`) scales *only
the trailing translation entries* by `ratio` and leaves the rotation / linear block untouched. The reason
is dimensional, and it is the same split the Lie algebra makes:

- **translations** are in voxel units, so they scale with the grid ratio;
- **the linear block** (rotation, scale, shear) is *dimensionless* - a rotation by $\omega$ is the same
  physical rotation at any resolution - so it is grid-independent and carries over unchanged.

The driver only applies this when the space asks for it. `IndexSpace` (`requires_grid_rescale = True`)
reads $\theta$ in voxel units and so must rescale; [`WorldSpace`](resampling.qmd#sec-pyramid)
(`requires_grid_rescale = False`) reads $\theta$ in physical millimetres, which are grid-independent by
construction, so its parameters carry over verbatim with no rescale at all. (The pyramid level mapping
itself is `align-corners`: a level-$\ell$ voxel index maps to the full grid by the pure scale
$(\text{full}-1)/(\text{level}-1)$, computed on host as a NumPy constant in `_grid_scale`.)

### Do not coarsen below ~16 voxels per axis

There is a floor on how coarse you can usefully go. The second-order step (@sec-gauss-newton) estimates
curvature from an image Hessian; on a grid of only a few voxels per axis that estimate degrades - there
simply are not enough voxels to pin down a 12-parameter affine reliably, and the step can overshoot into
a wrong, *anti-correlated* minimum (the parameters blow up and the warped image ends up negatively
correlated with the fixed). The recipes guard this with `_MIN_COARSE_AXIS = 16` (`register/recipes.py`):
`_cap_levels` shortens the *affine* pyramid (loudly, via an `AffinePyramidDepthWarning`) so the coarsest
level keeps $\ge 16$ voxels per axis. For example a 28³ image at the default 3 levels would drop its
coarsest level to $\approx 14^3$ and diverge; capping it to fewer levels keeps the coarsest grid
reliable. This is a targeted fix - it needs no extra damping, so the well-conditioned path is untouched -
and it is affine-only: the 6-DOF rigid Hessian is robust even at small coarse grids. See
`register-affine-small-grid-divergence.md` for the full diagnosis (the inverse-compositional path takes a
complementary trust-region cure, @sec-inverse-comp).

## Gauss–Newton: earning a second-order step from a residual {#sec-gauss-newton}

Now the core solver for the SSD case. Start from the obvious thing and improve it. We want to minimise

$$
C(\theta) = \tfrac12 \big\lVert r(\theta) \big\rVert^2,
\qquad
r(\theta) = I_m\!\circ\!\varphi_\theta - I_f
$$ {#eq-ssd-cost}

- the SSD cost is half the squared norm of the *residual vector* $r$, the per-voxel intensity difference
between the warped moving image and the fixed image. The gradient of @eq-ssd-cost is $\nabla C = J^\top
r$, where $J = \partial r / \partial \theta$ is the $M \times P$ Jacobian ($M$ voxels, $P$ parameters).
The obvious move - *gradient descent*, $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha J^\top r$ - is the first rung,
and it is slow: it ignores curvature, so on a cost surface where one parameter direction is far stiffer
than another (rotation vs translation, exactly our case) it zig-zags down the soft direction and crawls
along the stiff one.

The fix uses the *structure* of @eq-ssd-cost. Because the cost is a sum of squares, we can *linearise the
residual* rather than the cost. Near the current $\theta$,

$$
r(\theta + \delta) \approx r(\theta) + J\,\delta .
$$ {#eq-linearise}

Substitute that linear model into the cost and minimise over the step $\delta$: $\tfrac12 \lVert r +
J\delta\rVert^2$ is a *quadratic* in $\delta$, and setting its gradient to zero gives the *Gauss–Newton
normal equations*

$$
\big(J^\top J\big)\,\delta = -\,J^\top r .
$$ {#eq-normal}

The step $\delta$ is a vector in the Lie-algebra parameters, applied to the running transform through the
$\exp$ chart - so each Gauss–Newton iteration is a move in the flat tangent space, exactly the
unconstrained search the chart was built for. The matrix $J^\top J$ is the *Gauss–Newton approximation to
the Hessian* - it discards the second-derivative-of-residual term that the true Hessian carries, which is
exactly the term that is small when the residuals are small (near alignment) or the model is near-linear.
So Gauss–Newton is "second-order for free": it captures the dominant curvature using only the first-order
Jacobian, and near the solution it converges *quadratically* where gradient descent crawls.

@fig-optimization-gn shows the payoff on a registration-like stiff quadratic bowl. Gradient descent (with
the best stable constant step) zig-zags and crawls; Gauss–Newton, which solves @eq-normal exactly, jumps
to the minimiser of the local quadratic - one step on a quadratic, a handful on a real registration.

```{python}
#| label: fig-optimization-gn
#| fig-cap: "Gauss–Newton vs plain gradient descent on a stiff (ill-conditioned) quadratic bowl — the local picture of a registration step. Left: trajectories from the same start; gradient descent crawls down the stiff direction, Gauss–Newton (solving the normal equations) reaches the minimum in one step. Right: cost gap vs iteration (log scale) — GN drops to machine precision immediately; gradient descent decays slowly."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A registration-like least-squares bowl: cost = 1/2 p^T A p - b^T p, with A =
# J^T J badly conditioned (linear-block directions O(grid), translation O(1)),
# so gradient descent crawls along the stiff direction.
A = np.array([[40.0, 0.0], [0.0, 1.2]])     # J^T J, condition number ~33
b = np.array([40.0 * 3.0, 1.2 * 2.0])       # minimiser at p* = (3, 2)
p_star = np.linalg.solve(A, b)

def cost(p): return 0.5 * p @ A @ p - b @ p
def grad(p): return A @ p - b

# Gradient descent at the largest stable constant step (1/L).
L = np.linalg.eigvalsh(A).max()
lr = 1.0 / L
p = np.array([-4.0, 8.0]); gd_path = [p.copy()]
for _ in range(60):
    p = p - lr * grad(p); gd_path.append(p.copy())
gd_path = np.asarray(gd_path)

# Gauss-Newton: each step solves (J^T J) delta = -J^T r exactly.
p = np.array([-4.0, 8.0]); gn_path = [p.copy()]
for _ in range(60):
    p = p + np.linalg.solve(A, -grad(p)); gn_path.append(p.copy())
gn_path = np.asarray(gn_path)

def gap(path): return np.array([cost(q) for q in path]) - cost(p_star)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.0))
gx = np.linspace(-6, 6, 200); gy = np.linspace(-2, 9, 200)
GX, GY = np.meshgrid(gx, gy)
Z = 0.5 * (A[0, 0] * GX ** 2 + A[1, 1] * GY ** 2) - (b[0] * GX + b[1] * GY)
axL.contour(GX, GY, Z, levels=25, colors="0.8", linewidths=0.6)
axL.plot(gd_path[:, 0], gd_path[:, 1], "-o", ms=2.5, lw=1.0,
         color="#b03b4b", label="gradient descent")
axL.plot(gn_path[:, 0], gn_path[:, 1], "-o", ms=5, lw=1.4,
         color="#3b6fb0", label="Gauss-Newton")
axL.plot(*p_star, "k*", ms=12, label="minimiser")
axL.set_xlabel(r"parameter $\theta_1$ (stiff)")
axL.set_ylabel(r"parameter $\theta_2$ (soft)")
axL.set_title("Trajectories on a stiff quadratic bowl")
axL.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)

axR.semilogy(np.maximum(gap(gd_path), 1e-16), color="#b03b4b", lw=2,
             label="gradient descent")
axR.semilogy(np.maximum(gap(gn_path), 1e-16), color="#3b6fb0", lw=2,
             marker="o", ms=4, label="Gauss-Newton")
axR.set_xlabel("iteration"); axR.set_ylabel("cost above minimum")
axR.set_xlim(0, 40); axR.set_ylim(1e-16, 1e3)
axR.set_title("Iterations to converge")
axR.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Levenberg–Marquardt: damping the step

Gauss–Newton's confidence is misplaced when the start is poor: the linear model @eq-linearise is only
local, and a full Gauss–Newton step can overshoot. The classical cure is one extra idea - *interpolate
between Gauss–Newton and gradient descent* by adding a diagonal ridge $\lambda$:

$$
\big(J^\top J + \lambda I\big)\,\delta = -\,J^\top r .
$$ {#eq-lm}

When $\lambda \to 0$ this is Gauss–Newton (full, fast steps near the solution); when $\lambda$ is large
the step shrinks toward $-\tfrac1\lambda J^\top r$, a short, safe gradient-descent step.
*Levenberg–Marquardt* adapts $\lambda$: try the step, *accept* it only if the cost decreased, and shrink
$\lambda$ toward Gauss–Newton on accept or grow it toward gradient descent on reject. The accept/reject
keeps the cost trace monotone - a robustness guarantee Gauss–Newton lacks.

### The solvers in `linalg.optimize`

`gauss_newton` and `levenberg_marquardt` (`linalg/optimize.py`) implement exactly @eq-normal / @eq-lm.
Both are **DIFF-JAX** (jittable and reverse-differentiable). Two design choices make them GPU-native:

- **The fixed loop is rolled with `lax.scan`,** not Python-unrolled, so cold-compile time is roughly
  *constant* in `n_iters` (one iteration is compiled, not `n_iters` copies). It stays differentiable, so
  `jax.grad` through a registration works out of the box.
- **The normal-equation solve is matrix-free conjugate gradients** (`linalg.cg`, wrapping
  `jax.scipy.sparse.linalg.cg`). CG needs only matrix-vector products and inner products - no
  factorisation - so it never touches the cuSolver handle pool and stays on the GPU on stacks where a
  Cholesky / `solve` would wedge and force a host round-trip.

The Jacobian itself has two strategies, resolved by parameter count $P$ (`_resolve_jacobian`, threshold
`_ASSEMBLE_MAX_P = 64`). For the small $P$ of the rigid/affine regime ($P \le 12$) it *assembles* the
explicit $P \times P$ Gram matrix $J^\top J$ and runs CG on that tiny dense system (cheaper than a
matrix-free matvec there). For a large $P$ it goes *matrix-free*: the operator $v \mapsto J^\top(Jv)$ is
built from a cached `jax.linearize` (for $Jv$) and its `jax.linear_transpose` (for $J^\top u$), so the
$M \times P$ Jacobian is never materialised.

### The warp Jacobian: the chain-rule factorisation

Forming the Jacobian $J = \partial r / \partial \theta$ naively with `jax.jacfwd` means one forward-mode
pass *per parameter* - and each pass gathers a full warp tangent (an interpolation of the moving image).
That is $P$ gathers, the expensive part. `register/_core.py`'s `_warp_jacobian` computes the same
Jacobian *exactly* but with far fewer gathers, by factoring the chain rule. The warp is "build a sampling
grid from $\theta$, then resample the moving image at that grid", so

$$
J[x, j]
= \frac{\partial\,\text{warp}(x)}{\partial \theta_j}
= \underbrace{\frac{\partial\,\text{warp}(x)}{\partial\,\text{grid}}}_{\text{interpolation derivative}}
\cdot
\underbrace{\frac{\partial\,\text{grid}(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}}_{\text{grid Jacobian}} .
$$ {#eq-warp-jac}

The two factors have completely different cost (@fig-optimization-chain). The left factor
$\partial\,\text{warp}/\partial\,\text{grid}$ is the *image gradient resampled* - it touches the image,
but it is an $M \times d$ object ($d = \text{ndim}$ grid axes), computed with *one* JVP per axis: *`ndim`
gathers, not `P`*. The right factor $\partial\,\text{grid}/\partial\theta$ is the derivative of the grid
*construction* - it is the derivative of the $\exp$ chart composed with the centred-affine grid, pure
matmul, no gather - taken one parameter-column at a time and contracted into the left factor immediately,
so the dense $(*\text{spatial}, d, P)$ grid tangent `jacfwd` would materialise never exists. Peak memory
is the $(M, P)$ Jacobian itself plus one $(*\text{spatial}, d)$ column at a time. The result is
*byte-identical* to `jax.jacfwd` of the SSD residual (it is the exact interpolation derivative, not a
finite difference) - just faster, with the gather cut from $O(P)$ to $O(\text{ndim})$. The driver passes
this as `jacobian_fn`; `None` falls back to `jacfwd` as a parity oracle. (`_mask_jacobian` then row-scales
it by $\sqrt{\text{mask}}$ so an out-of-mask voxel contributes a zero row and drops out of the normal
equations, matching `SSD.residual`'s masked form.)

```{python}
#| label: fig-optimization-chain
#| fig-cap: "The warp-Jacobian chain rule. Factoring ∂warp/∂θ into (∂warp/∂grid)·(∂grid/∂θ) separates the one expensive piece — the interpolation derivative, an image gather run once per grid axis (ndim of them) — from the cheap grid-construction derivative (matmul, one column per parameter). This cuts the gathers from O(P) (jax.jacfwd: a warp tangent per parameter) to O(ndim)."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.6, 3.4))
ax.axis("off")

def box(x, y, w, h, text, fc, ec="0.3"):
    ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((x, y), w, h, fc=fc, ec=ec, lw=1.3, zorder=2))
    ax.text(x + w / 2, y + h / 2, text, ha="center", va="center",
            fontsize=9, zorder=3)

def arrow(x0, y0, x1, y1):
    ax.annotate("", xy=(x1, y1), xytext=(x0, y0),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", lw=1.6, color="0.25"), zorder=1)

box(0.02, 0.55, 0.24, 0.30,
    r"$\dfrac{\partial\,\mathrm{grid}}{\partial\theta_j}$" + "\n"
    "(d x P) per column\nmatmul only -- no gather", "#dfe9f5")
ax.text(0.14, 0.92, "build the sampling grid", ha="center", fontsize=8, color="0.4")
box(0.38, 0.55, 0.26, 0.30,
    r"$\dfrac{\partial\,\mathrm{warp}}{\partial\,\mathrm{grid}}$" + "\n"
    "(M x d) = image gradient\nndim gathers (one / axis)", "#f3e2cf")
ax.text(0.51, 0.92, "resample the moving image", ha="center", fontsize=8, color="0.4")
box(0.76, 0.55, 0.22, 0.30,
    r"$\dfrac{\partial\,\mathrm{warp}}{\partial\theta}$" + "\n"
    "(M x P)\nresidual Jacobian", "#dCEFD9")
arrow(0.265, 0.70, 0.375, 0.70)
ax.text(0.32, 0.74, "contract\nover d", ha="center", fontsize=7.5, color="0.3")
arrow(0.645, 0.70, 0.755, 0.70)
ax.text(0.5, 0.30,
        "gathers cut from O(P) (jax.jacfwd: one warp-tangent per parameter)\n"
        "to O(ndim) (one interpolation-derivative pass per grid axis)",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=9.5,
        bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.5", fc="#f7f7f7", ec="0.6"))
ax.set_xlim(0, 1); ax.set_ylim(0.1, 1.0)
ax.set_title("The warp-Jacobian chain rule", fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Differentiating *through* the registration

Sometimes you want a gradient of a downstream loss with respect to the *images* being registered
(registration as a differentiable layer). Two routes exist. The fixed `lax.scan` loop is already
reverse-differentiable, so `jax.grad` through an unrolled solve works - at the memory cost of storing
every iterate. The better route is `implicit_least_squares` (and its scalar-objective sibling
`implicit_minimize`), both **DIFF-JAX**, which differentiate at the *converged* optimum via the
implicit-function theorem rather than through the trajectory. At the optimum the stationarity $J^\top r =
0$ gives $\partial x^* / \partial \text{data} = -(J^\top J)^{-1} \partial_{\text{data}} g$; the backward
solves $(J^\top J)\,w = \bar x$ with the same matrix-free CG. Memory is $O(1)$ in the iteration count and
$O(M + P)$ overall - neither the $M \times P$ Jacobian nor the $P \times P$ normal matrix is
materialised. This is the path to use when you need gradients and the early-exit loop (@sec-convergence)
would otherwise refuse them.

## BFGS: when there is no residual {#sec-bfgs}

The Gauss–Newton machinery rests entirely on the residual vector - it *is* the thing $J$ differentiates.
A scalar metric has none. [LNCC](similarity.qmd#sec-lncc) normalises by local windows,
[MI](similarity.qmd#sec-mi) and [correlation-ratio](similarity.qmd#sec-cr) are functionals of a joint
histogram; none of them is $\tfrac12\lVert r\rVert^2$ for any natural $r$. So `is_least_squares` is
`False`, and the dispatch in `optimize_objective` (`register/_core.py`) routes to *BFGS* via
`jax.scipy.optimize.minimize(method='BFGS')`.

BFGS is a first-order quasi-Newton method: it builds an *estimate* of the inverse Hessian from the
history of gradients and steps, so it captures curvature (and so handles the rotation/translation scaling
sensibly) without ever forming a Hessian or needing a residual. Contrast it with Gauss–Newton: when you
*have* a residual, Gauss–Newton hands you the curvature ($J^\top J$) directly and exactly -
second-order-ish for free, in a handful of steps - whereas BFGS must *learn* the curvature over many
iterations from gradient differences. That is the price of the scalar metrics' generality, and the reason
SSD is the fast default.

The BFGS path is also why `mode='early_exit'` is rejected on the scalar metrics:
`jax.scipy.optimize.minimize` is monolithic - there is no exposed single step to gate - so the
convergence machinery (@sec-convergence) has nothing to hook into. The `optimize_objective` dispatch
carries a defensive guard, but the real gate lives upstream in `resolve_convergence_mode`.

## The inverse-compositional fast path {#sec-inverse-comp}

Even with Gauss–Newton, the forward path re-linearises the *warped moving* image every iteration: each
step needs $J$, and $J$ depends on $\theta$ (the moving image is resampled at the current grid, then
differentiated). For a 3-D affine that is $\approx 14$ tangent warps *per iteration*. The
*inverse-compositional* scheme (Baker–Matthews, 2004), in `register/_inverse_compositional.py`, removes
almost all of that cost with one observation.

The forward residual is $r = I_m\!\circ\!W(\theta) - I_f$, and its sensitivity sits on the *moving* side,
which moves every iteration. Instead, *linearise the template (fixed image) and absorb the update on the
moving side compositionally.* The steepest-descent images

$$
\mathrm{SD}[x, j] = \nabla I_f(x) \cdot \frac{\partial W(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}\bigg|_{\theta=0}
$$ {#eq-sd}

are evaluated *at the identity, on the fixed image* - so they do *not* depend on the current iterate, and
neither does the Gauss–Newton Hessian $H = \mathrm{SD}^\top \mathrm{SD}$. Both are computed *once* per
pyramid level and *reused every iteration* (`_steepest_descent` / `_affine_steepest_descent` build the SD
columns; `_hessian_inv` builds $H^{-1}$; `ic_reference` precomputes them all). Per iteration only the
cheap part runs: one warp, the error $e = I_m\!\circ\!T - I_f$, the projection $\mathrm{SD}^\top e$, an
$H^{-1}$ matvec, and a matrix update. One warp per iteration vs the forward path's many - about 4–7×
faster.

The update is where "inverse-compositional" earns its name, and it is a group operation in the precise
sense of the [transforms chapter](transforms.qmd#sec-transform-manifold). Because we linearised the
template, the increment $\Delta\theta$ describes a warp of the *template*, so it is composed onto the
running transform *inverted*:

$$
T \leftarrow T \cdot W(\Delta\theta)^{-1}.
$$ {#eq-ic-update}

The recovered warp is carried as the homogeneous matrix $T$ (fixed-voxel → moving-voxel) throughout,
never re-parametrised - the iteration walks the group directly by right-multiplication. The inverse is
the group inverse, and because the group has a closed-form one it stays GPU-native: for rigid,
$W(\Delta\theta)^{-1}$ uses the rotation-transpose rule $R^{-1} = R^\top$ (`_rigid_inverse`, no solve);
for affine, the linear-block inverse is $\text{matrix\_exp}(-A)$ (`_affine_compositional_update`, pure
matmul, since $\exp(A)^{-1}=\exp(-A)$ in the group). The Lie structure is doing real work here: the
inverse of a group element is free precisely because the chart is the exponential.

A clean efficiency detail: the per-iteration projection $\mathrm{SD}^\top e$ is *not* read from a stored
$(M, P)$ steepest-descent buffer (that would keep $P$ copies of the image resident). It is reconstructed
from the *moments* of the fixed-image gradient - $m_{ij} = \sum_x \nabla I_f{}_i\,(x-c)_j\,e$ and
$g_i = \sum_x \nabla I_f{}_i\,e$ - and `project_moments` assembles the model's $\mathrm{SD}^\top e$ from
them (rigid contracts $m$ against the $\mathfrak{so}(n)$ generators; affine flattens it). So
`ic_reference` keeps only $\nabla I_f$ resident, a $P/\text{ndim}$ memory saving (4× at 3-D affine).

### Trust region and the line-search guard

The constant-template Hessian is the source of the speed *and* of a hazard: it is built once from the
whole reference, so on a few-voxel coarse grid or a low-gradient image it can be unreliable, and the raw
Gauss–Newton step can overshoot until `matrix_exp` explodes (parameters → thousands, the warp
anti-correlates - the same small-grid failure mode as @sec-coarse-fine). Rather than damp *every* step
(which would slow the well-conditioned path and throttle early-exit), the kernel clamps only the
pathological step. `_trust_scale` computes the step's *induced grid displacement* - how far the worst grid
corner moves under $T \to T\,W(\Delta\theta)$ - and shortens the step if that exceeds the grid's own extent
(`_TRUST_EXTENT_FACTOR = 1.0` × the grid extent). The rationale is physical: you cannot align two images
by moving the sampling grid farther than the image is wide, so such a step is meaningless and is bounded.
A normal step (displacement $\le$ extent) is left *byte-unchanged*, so the well-conditioned path keeps its
single-step Gauss–Newton convergence and the Hessian ridge stays tiny (a Jacobi-preconditioned relative
ridge `_RIDGE = 1e-4`, which `_hessian_inv` applies to a unit-diagonal-scaled Hessian so the affine
block's order-of-magnitude column spread does not mis-damp the translation directions).

The opt-in `ic_line_search` (`RegistrationSpec.ic_line_search`, default `False`) adds a second guard:
even a trust-clamped step from the constant-template Hessian can point in a slightly *wrong direction*
and increase the SSD on a hard case. With the guard on, the step is *backtracked* along the clamped
direction over the fractions `_BACKTRACK_ALPHAS = (1.0, 0.5, 0.25)`, accepting the largest that decreases
the cost; if none does, the iterate is left unmoved - so the per-level cost is *monotone non-increasing*.
A step that already decreases at full length (the well-conditioned case) is taken byte-unchanged at
$\alpha = 1$. The guard costs extra candidate warps (~3× the IC step), so it is off by default and
enabled for robustness on pathological data.

### Where the fast path applies

The public entry points are `ic_rigid_register` and `ic_affine_register` (single-pair), built on the
shared `ic_reference` (precompute the per-level reference data) and `ic_register_core` (run the
coarse-to-fine IC iterations, carrying the matrix). The transform group enters as three small callables
passed to `ic_register_core` - `compositional_update` ($W(\Delta\theta)^{-1}$), `params_from_matrix`,
and `project_moments` - rigid by default, affine for the affine recipe. The constraints are firm and
follow from the construction: *SSD only* (it is a least-squares scheme - there is a residual to
linearise) and *`IndexSpace` only* (the template is linearised in voxel coordinates, with no conjugating
change-of-basis). `rigid_register` / `affine_register` dispatch here automatically under `method='auto'`
when those preconditions hold (and an unmasked cost - a mask needs a per-voxel reduction the full-grid
Hessian cannot honour); the forward Gauss–Newton path is the parity oracle the fast path is validated
against, so the two are interchangeable.

## Convergence: fixed scan vs early-exit while-loop {#sec-convergence}

The last shared piece is *how many iterations to run*, factored into `register/_converge.py` and consumed
by both the matrix IC path and the dense-field SVF drivers. There are two strategies, and they trade off
reproducibility/differentiability against speed.

The strategy is `RegistrationSpec.mode`, a `ConvergenceMode` (`'fixed'` or `'early_exit'`), and
`run_iterations(step_fn, init_state, …, convergence)` is the one function that runs either:

- **`mode='fixed'`** (the spec default on every recipe) runs a fixed-length `lax.scan` over the level's
  iteration count. It is *reproducible*, `vmap`-batchable (so `volreg` can register a whole series at
  once), and *reverse-differentiable*. The scan body is wrapped in `jax.checkpoint`, so reverse-mode
  through the unrolled trajectory *rematerialises* each step on the backward pass instead of storing every
  iterate - `grad` memory is $O(\text{state})$ + recompute rather than $O(\text{iterations} \times
  \text{state})$ (the dense-field SVF carry is large). `checkpoint` is a no-op on the forward pass, so the
  common grad-free forward result and its compile are byte-unchanged.

- **`mode='early_exit'`** runs a `lax.while_loop` that stops once the cost has plateaued - an ANTs-style
  criterion: fit a least-squares line over the last `window` per-iteration costs, normalise its slope by
  the mean cost, and stop when $|\text{slope}| / |\text{mean}| < \text{threshold}$ (the `Convergence`
  record's `threshold` / `window`). The iteration count is the hard cap. The cost trace is padded to that
  cap with the final cost, so `cost_history` keeps a *path-independent shape* either way. On a hard rigid
  warp this converges in ~18 of 90 iterations (≈1.7× faster) with identical recovery - single-pair
  inverse-compositional is the recipe class it is shipped for.

### The caveat: no reverse rule through `while_loop`

There is a hard constraint on the early-exit path, and the code raises it loudly. *A `lax.while_loop` has
no reverse-mode rule* - the data-dependent trip count cannot be back-propagated. So reverse-mode
(`jax.grad`) through an `early_exit` registration is unsupported. The code does not let this fail
obscurely: the while-loop output passes through `_early_exit_barrier`, a `jax.custom_vjp` identity on the
forward pass whose backward rule *raises a registration-specific, actionable error* pointing you to the
fix - use `mode='fixed'` (the reverse-differentiable scan) or the implicit-function layer
([`implicit_least_squares` / `implicit_minimize`](#sec-gauss-newton)), whose adjoint is solved at the
optimum and is trajectory-independent. The `convergence=None` (fixed-scan) path never touches the
barrier, so it stays differentiable.

Two more behavioural notes. The eligibility gate `resolve_convergence_mode` is the single place the mode
decision is resolved: it returns `None` for the fixed scan, or the concrete `Convergence` for early-exit,
and *raises* if `mode='early_exit'` is asked of a path that cannot honour a data-dependent trip count (the
scalar/BFGS forward optimiser, which is monolithic). And a `vmap`-ed `while_loop` *does* batch - but it
runs until the *slowest lane* exits (the all-lanes condition), so the per-pair early-exit advantage
degrades to the cohort's slowest pair. That is why `volreg` uses the fixed scan: its batched-GPU
throughput is the structural win, not per-frame early exit.

## Further reading

- B. D. Lucas and T. Kanade (1981). *An iterative image registration technique with an application to
  stereo vision.* IJCAI. The forward additive scheme the SSD path descends from.
- S. Baker and I. Matthews (2004). *Lucas–Kanade 20 years on: a unifying framework.* International Journal
  of Computer Vision 56(3):221–255. The inverse-compositional algorithm of @sec-inverse-comp, with the
  constant-template Hessian and the compositional inverse update.
- J. Nocedal and S. J. Wright (2006). *Numerical Optimization*, 2nd ed., Springer. Gauss–Newton,
  Levenberg–Marquardt (Ch. 10), BFGS (Ch. 6), and trust regions (Ch. 4).
- K. Levenberg (1944) and D. W. Marquardt (1963). The damped least-squares step of @eq-lm.
- M. Jaderberg, K. Simonyan, A. Zisserman, K. Kavukcuoglu (2015). *Spatial Transformer Networks.* NeurIPS.
  Differentiable resampling, the substrate the analytic warp Jacobian and the implicit-differentiation
  layer rest on.
- S. Gould et al. (2016/2021) and A. Agrawal et al. (2019). *Differentiating through argmin / implicit
  layers.* The implicit-function-theorem path of `implicit_least_squares` / `implicit_minimize`.
- B. B. Avants, N. J. Tustison, et al. (2011). *A reproducible evaluation of ANTs similarity metric
  performance in brain image registration.* NeuroImage 54(3):2033–2044. The multi-resolution +
  windowed-convergence lineage nitrix matches in behaviour.
- R. W. Cox and A. Jesmanowicz (1999). *Real-time 3D image registration for functional MRI.* Magnetic
  Resonance in Medicine 42:1014–1018. AFNI `3dvolreg`, the realignment lineage of the SSD /
  inverse-compositional path.

## See also

- Source — driver and objective:
  [`register/_core.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_core.py),
  [`register/_objective.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_objective.py),
  [`register/_model.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_model.py),
  [`register/_space.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_space.py).
- Source — solvers and convergence:
  [`linalg/optimize.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/optimize.py),
  [`linalg/krylov.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/krylov.py),
  [`register/_converge.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_converge.py),
  [`register/_inverse_compositional.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_inverse_compositional.py),
  [`register/recipes.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/recipes.py).
- Neighbouring pages: [the transform charts and Lie exponential](transforms.qmd#sec-lie-exp),
  [resampling and the Gaussian pyramid](resampling.qmd#sec-pyramid),
  [the similarity metrics](similarity.qmd#sec-ssd),
  [the diffeomorphic / SVF solvers](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-svf) (which share this convergence
  scaffolding), and [the recipes and specs](recipes.qmd#sec-specs).
